content.problemStatement:
In the second part of the Clustering & Dimensionality Reduction project, we will dive into the powerful technique of unsupervised learning using K-Means clustering. Building on the insights we gained in the previous project, we will apply K-Means clustering on the Mall Customers Dataset, which includes features such as age, annual income, and spending score.

**You will:**
1. Standardize the data: Begin by selecting the relevant features - Age, Annual Income, and Spending Score - and standardize them using the StandardScaler to ensure that each feature contributes equally to the clustering algorithm.

2. Apply K-Means clustering: We will experiment with different values of k (the number of clusters), ranging from 2 to 10. For each value of k, we will compute the inertia, which measures how well the points fit within their assigned clusters.

3. Evaluate clustering quality: To further evaluate the effectiveness of each clustering, we will compute the Silhouette Score for each value of k and identify which k produces the best clustering results.

```text
Dataset shape: (200, 5)
Best k by silhouette score: 6
```
This project will give you hands-on experience with one of the most widely used clustering algorithms, K-Means, and help you understand its evaluation techniques. In Part 3, we will take a step further and explore Dimensionality Reduction with PCA, while also using it for a classification task on a completely different dataset, the MNIST digits dataset.

### **Helpful Resources**
- [Pandas Documentation](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/)
- [StandardScaler](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.preprocessing.StandardScaler.html)
- [silhouette_score](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.metrics.silhouette_score.html)
- [K-means](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/clustering.html#k-means)
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
